title: What to do with a poet's library
summary: A famous poet has died. His executor wants to break up the library; an archivist argues that the library, as a set, is itself a second body of work. They negotiate a third option neither came with.

body.speaker: Helena
body.text: Mr Dao, thank you for coming. As his executor I have a decision to make, and I would rather make it after talking to you. The library — eight thousand volumes — what would you do?

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: Forgive me if I begin with a question. What do you think the library is?

body.speaker: Helena
body.text: Eight thousand books, organised by language and period, with a catalogue. Worth perhaps three hundred million dong at auction. Less if sold by weight.

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: That is what the library contains. It is not what the library is.

body.speaker: Helena
body.text: You will need to be less Socratic with me. We are running short on time.

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: I'll be direct. The library is not the books. It is the books in that specific order, in that house, with his handwritten notes in the margins. Break it up, and you destroy something that cannot be re-assembled.

body.speaker: Helena
body.text: And you think this 'something' matters more than twenty new readers having his books in their own homes?

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: I think it matters more than the assumption that those are the only two options. A complete poet's library is, in some sense, a second body of work. Scholars will study it for fifty years. They do not get to perform that study if the library has been scattered.

body.speaker: Helena
body.text: But who actually reads these books in the long term? If the library stays whole and becomes an archive, the books sit behind glass. They serve no one.

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: That is the strongest version of your argument, and it is fair. But the answer is not to scatter. It is to make the archive usable. The Italians do this with composer libraries — anyone can request a score, on the desk, where the composer wrote on it. It is not glass.

body.speaker: Helena
body.text: And if I, as executor, do not have the budget for that?

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: Then we have a more honest conversation about money, not about meaning. But before any book moves, allow me one thing. Photograph the shelves as they stand. Every shelf, in order. Photograph the margins of the annotated books. Make a digital record of the order itself.

body.speaker: Helena
body.text: And after that record exists, you would accept that some books could leave?

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: Some, yes. The unmarked novels he never opened. A few duplicates. The annotated, the dedicated, the books he wrote his own poems inside — those should stay together.

body.speaker: Helena
body.text: I came in thinking I had two choices: keep everything, or sell it all. You are offering me a third.

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: You came in thinking the library was a problem. I am asking you to consider that it is also a witness. It saw him become who he was. Witnesses should be kept around.

body.speaker: Mr Dao
body.text: Yes. Available to whoever brings the right question.

annotations.meaningVi: người thi hành di chúc, người quản lý di sản
kiểu Socrates — đặt câu hỏi để dẫn dắt thay vì nói thẳng
bị phân tán, rải rác mỗi nơi một ít
có ghi chú, có chú thích viết tay

grammarNotes.title: Defining by contrast: 'X is not A; it is B'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Mẫu câu định nghĩa bằng cách phủ định và đối chiếu — 'It is not X; it is Y' — giúp làm rõ điều người nói muốn nói bằng cách loại trừ một hiểu lầm phổ biến. Trong đoạn thoại: 'The library is not the books. It is the books in that specific order, in that house, with his handwritten notes in the margins.' Ông Đào không nói 'thư viện không có sách' — ông định nghĩa lại từ 'library' bằng cách thêm bối cảnh và loại trừ định nghĩa hời hợt.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The pattern 'X is not A; it is B' (or 'not X, but Y') defines something by ruling out a common misunderstanding. From the dialogue: 'The library is not the books. It is the books in that specific order, in that house, with his handwritten notes in the margins.' Mr Dao isn't denying the library has books — he is redefining the term by adding context and rejecting a shallow definition.

cloze.template:
Helena: Mr Dao, thank you for coming. As his {{b1}} I have a decision to make, and I would rather make it after talking to you. The library — eight {{b2}} volumes — what would you do?
Mr Dao: Forgive me if I begin with a question. What do you think the library is?
Helena: Eight thousand books, organised by language and period, with a catalogue. Worth perhaps three hundred million dong at auction. Less if sold by weight.
Mr Dao: That is what the library contains. It is not what the library is.
Helena: You will need to be less {{b3}} with me. We are running short on time.
Mr Dao: I'll be direct. The library is not the books. It is the books in that specific order, in that house, with his handwritten notes in the {{b4}}. Break it up, and you destroy something that cannot be {{b5}}.
Helena: And you think this 'something' matters more than twenty new readers having his books in their own homes?
Mr Dao: I think it matters more than the assumption that those are the only two options. A complete poet's library is, in some sense, a second body of work. {{b6}} will study it for fifty years. They do not get to perform that study if the library has been {{b7}}.
Helena: But who actually reads these books in the long term? If the library stays whole and becomes an {{b8}}, the books sit behind glass. They serve no one.
Mr Dao: That is the strongest version of your argument, and it is fair. But the answer is not to scatter. It is to make the archive usable. The Italians do this with {{b9}} libraries — anyone can request a score, on the desk, where the composer wrote on it. It is not glass.
Helena: And if I, as executor, do not have the {{b10}} for that?
Mr Dao: Then we have a more honest conversation about money, not about meaning. But before any book moves, allow me one thing. Photograph the shelves as they stand. Every shelf, in order. Photograph the margins of the annotated books. Make a {{b11}} record of the order itself.
Helena: And after that record exists, you would accept that some books could leave?
Mr Dao: Some, yes. The unmarked novels he never opened. A few {{b12}}. The {{b13}}, the dedicated, the books he wrote his own poems inside — those should stay together.
Helena: I came in thinking I had two choices: keep everything, or sell it all. You are offering me a third.
Mr Dao: You came in thinking the library was a problem. I am asking you to consider that it is also a {{b14}}. It saw him become who he was. Witnesses should be kept around.
Helena: Witnesses without {{b15}}, you mean.
Mr Dao: Yes. Available to whoever brings the right {{b16}}.

blanks.explanation: An executor is the person legally responsible for carrying out a will after someone dies.
Eight thousand volumes — the size of the poet's library.
Helena complains that Mr Dao is being Socratic — asking questions instead of answering directly.
Handwritten notes in the margins of the books — the white space at the edges of each page.
Break the library up and you destroy something that cannot be re-assembled — put back together as it was.
Scholars (people who study a subject deeply) are the ones who would use the intact library for fifty years.
Scattered — spread out among many different owners, so no single study is possible.
An archive — a place where documents are kept for study. Helena's worry is that an archive serves no one.
Composer libraries — Italian collections of musicians' personal scores, made directly usable.
Helena's practical question: she may not have the budget (money set aside) for a reading room and staff.
A digital record — photographs and data stored electronically — preserves the shelf order even if the books are eventually moved.
Duplicates — copies of the same book — can leave the library without harming the whole.
The annotated books — those with the poet's notes in the margins — should stay together with the dedicated and the books he wrote in.
Mr Dao reframes the library as a witness — something that 'saw him become who he was'.
Helena finishes the metaphor: witnesses without microphones — present but silent unless someone asks.
Available to whoever brings the right question — the library only speaks to those who know what to ask.

translationVi:
Helena: Ông Đào, cảm ơn ông đã đến. Là người thi hành di chúc, tôi phải đưa ra một quyết định, và tôi muốn quyết định sau khi nói chuyện với ông. Thư viện — tám nghìn cuốn — ông sẽ làm gì?
Ông Đào: Cho phép tôi mở đầu bằng một câu hỏi. Theo cô, thư viện ấy là gì?
Helena: Tám nghìn cuốn sách, sắp xếp theo ngôn ngữ và thời kỳ, có danh mục. Trị giá tối đa chừng ba trăm triệu đồng nếu đem đấu giá. Ít hơn nếu bán theo cân.
Ông Đào: Đó là cái mà thư viện chứa. Không phải cái mà thư viện là.
Helena: Ông sẽ cần bớt kiểu Socrates với tôi đi. Chúng ta không còn nhiều thời gian.
Ông Đào: Tôi sẽ nói thẳng. Thư viện không phải là sách. Nó là những cuốn sách ấy, ở thứ tự ấy, trong căn nhà ấy, với những ghi chú viết tay của ông ở lề. Tách nó ra, và cô phá hủy một thứ không thể ráp lại được.
Helena: Và ông cho rằng cái 'thứ ấy' quan trọng hơn việc hai mươi người đọc mới có sách của ông trong nhà họ?
Ông Đào: Tôi cho rằng nó quan trọng hơn cái giả định rằng đó là hai lựa chọn duy nhất. Một thư viện đầy đủ của một nhà thơ, ở khía cạnh nào đó, là một bộ tác phẩm thứ hai. Các học giả sẽ nghiên cứu nó suốt năm mươi năm. Họ không thể thực hiện việc đó nếu thư viện đã bị phân tán.
Helena: Nhưng về lâu dài thật sự ai đọc những cuốn sách này? Nếu thư viện được giữ nguyên và trở thành kho lưu trữ, sách nằm sau kính. Chúng chẳng phục vụ ai cả.
Ông Đào: Đó là phiên bản mạnh nhất của lập luận của cô, và nó công bằng. Nhưng câu trả lời không phải là phân tán. Mà là làm cho kho lưu trữ trở nên có thể dùng được. Người Ý làm vậy với các thư viện của nhạc sĩ — ai cũng có thể yêu cầu một bản nhạc, để trên bàn, ngay chỗ nhạc sĩ đã viết lên đó. Nó không phải đằng sau kính.
Helena: Và nếu tôi, với vai trò người thi hành, không có ngân sách cho việc đó?
Ông Đào: Thì chúng ta có một cuộc trò chuyện thành thật hơn về tiền, chứ không phải về ý nghĩa. Nhưng trước khi bất cứ cuốn sách nào di chuyển, cho phép tôi một điều. Hãy chụp ảnh các kệ sách như chúng đang là. Mỗi kệ, theo đúng thứ tự. Chụp các trang ghi chú của những cuốn sách có chú thích. Tạo một bản ghi số về chính thứ tự đó.
Helena: Và sau khi bản ghi đó tồn tại, ông có chấp nhận để một số sách rời đi?
Ông Đào: Một số, vâng. Những cuốn tiểu thuyết chưa từng được mở. Vài bản trùng. Còn những cuốn có ghi chú, những cuốn đề tặng, những cuốn ông viết thơ của mình bên trong — những cuốn đó nên ở cùng nhau.
Helena: Tôi đến đây nghĩ rằng mình có hai lựa chọn: giữ tất cả, hoặc bán tất cả. Ông đang đưa cho tôi một lựa chọn thứ ba.
Ông Đào: Cô đến đây nghĩ thư viện là một vấn đề. Tôi xin cô cân nhắc rằng nó cũng là một nhân chứng. Nó đã thấy ông trở thành chính ông. Nhân chứng thì nên được giữ lại.
Helena: Nhân chứng không có micro, ý ông là vậy.
Ông Đào: Vâng. Sẵn sàng cho bất kỳ ai mang đến đúng câu hỏi.

questions.prompt: Who is Helena, and why is she meeting Mr Dao?
options: A buyer; she wants to purchase the library
The poet's executor; she has a decision to make about his library
The poet's daughter inheriting the books
questions.explanation: 'As his executor I have a decision to make, and I would rather make it after talking to you.'

questions.prompt: How many books are in the library?
questions.explanation: 'Eight thousand volumes' — Helena names the size from the start.

questions.prompt: What is Mr Dao's first response to Helena's question?
options: He begins with another question: 'What do you think the library is?'
questions.explanation: 'Forgive me if I begin with a question. What do you think the library is?'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Dao's first turn.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Dao say the library 'is not'?
options: Just the books — it is the books in that order, in that house, with the poet's notes in the margins
questions.explanation: 'The library is not the books. It is the books in that specific order, in that house, with his handwritten notes in the margins.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Dao's turn after Helena calls him Socratic.

questions.prompt: Why does he say breaking the library up matters?
options: The whole is, in some sense, a second body of work; scattered, it cannot be studied
questions.explanation: 'A complete poet's library is, in some sense, a second body of work. Scholars will study it for fifty years.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Dao's reply about scholars.

questions.prompt: What is the strongest version of Helena's argument, in Mr Dao's words?
options: The library is too small to matter
If kept whole, books sit behind glass and serve no one
The poet would have wanted them sold
questions.explanation: Helena: 'The books sit behind glass. They serve no one.' Mr Dao calls this 'the strongest version of your argument'.
questions.hint: Look at Helena's turn beginning 'But who actually reads…'

questions.prompt: What example does Mr Dao give of a usable archive?
options: Italian composer libraries — anyone can request a score on the desk where the composer wrote on it
questions.explanation: 'The Italians do this with composer libraries — anyone can request a score, on the desk, where the composer wrote on it. It is not glass.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Dao's turn about Italians.

questions.prompt: What is Helena's practical worry about a usable archive?
options: She may not have the budget for staff and a reading room
questions.explanation: 'And if I, as executor, do not have the budget for that?'
questions.hint: Look at Helena's turn after Mr Dao's Italian example.

questions.prompt: What minimum does Mr Dao ask for before any book moves?
options: A meeting with all the scholars
A digital record — photographs of the shelves as they stand, in order, and of the annotated margins
Permission to keep all the books
questions.explanation: 'Photograph the shelves as they stand. Every shelf, in order. Photograph the margins of the annotated books. Make a digital record of the order itself.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Dao's turn after the budget question.

questions.prompt: Which books does Mr Dao agree could leave the library afterwards?
options: The annotated books and the ones the poet wrote in
The unmarked novels he never opened, and duplicates
questions.explanation: 'The unmarked novels he never opened. A few duplicates.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Dao's turn about which books could leave.

questions.prompt: Which books does Mr Dao say should stay together?
options: The annotated, the dedicated, and the books he wrote his own poems inside
questions.explanation: 'The annotated, the dedicated, the books he wrote his own poems inside — those should stay together.'
questions.hint: Same turn as the previous question — the contrast.

questions.prompt: What metaphor does Mr Dao use to describe the library at the end?
options: A witness — it saw him become who he was
questions.explanation: 'I am asking you to consider that it is also a witness. It saw him become who he was.'
questions.hint: Look at Mr Dao's second-to-last turn.

questions.prompt: What does Helena mean by 'witnesses without microphones'?
options: They are not allowed to speak in public
Witnesses present but silent — available only when someone asks the right question
They have been muted by the law
They have been replaced by recordings
questions.explanation: Mr Dao confirms: 'Yes. Available to whoever brings the right question.' The library does not announce itself.
questions.hint: Look at the final two turns.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is closer to Mr Dao's central position?
options: A library is just a list of items; its order does not matter.
A library's order, annotations, and house are themselves a 'second body of work' that cannot be re-assembled once scattered.
All books should always stay together forever.
Scholars are the only people who matter.
questions.explanation: His core claim is that the library is not its inventory but the relationship between the books.
questions.hint: Look at his definition of the library.

questions.prompt: Which of these is Mr Dao NOT claiming?
options: That scattering the library destroys something irreplaceable
That sitting behind glass is the only alternative to scattering
That a digital record of the shelf order should be made before any book moves
That some books can leave once the record exists
questions.explanation: Mr Dao explicitly rejects the 'glass' framing. He proposes a usable archive, not a sealed one.
questions.hint: Look at how he responds to Helena's 'sit behind glass' worry.

questions.prompt: What is the overall tone of the exchange?
options: Hostile — Helena and Mr Dao remain opposed
Respectful disagreement — each acknowledges something true in the other's position, and they arrive at a third option
questions.explanation: Mr Dao calls Helena's worry 'the strongest version of your argument, and it is fair'; Helena ends by accepting his digital-record proposal and saying he has offered her a third path.
questions.hint: Notice how each treats the other's argument before responding.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Dao says the library is a 'witness without a microphone' — it has seen something but speaks only when the right question is asked. Think of an object or place in your own life that holds a record of who you used to be. Would breaking it up — even into good homes — destroy something a record could not capture?
